sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 79, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'MOUSE on green object (N)': 'Moves selection ring (G) to clicked green object; gray ring (g) appears on an empty cell VERIFIED(step 1)', 'MOUSE on gray ring (empty cell at 19,12)': 'TRANSITION: removes all green objects, leaves 4 gray rings as target markers, creates purple/pink avatar at bottom-left VERIFIED(step 11)', 'MOUSE on empty cell (not gray ring)': 'Changes HUD element at top, no gameplay effect VERIFIED(step 10)', 'UP': 'Board changed but avatar not found in segmentation - may have moved or changed form VERIFIED(step 12)', 'RIGHT': 'No visible effect on green objects VERIFIED(step 2,8); testing with avatar now', 'ACTION7': "Execution failed with 'Unknown action' error VERIFIED(step 5)"}
- Object taxonomy: {'N': 'light green plus-shaped objects (12 pixels each, 5 total, hash=fa2a328d20606f99) - REMOVED after phase transition', 'G': 'dark gray selection ring on green object (20 pixels) - GONE after phase transition', 'g': 'gray ring markers (12 pixels each) at target positions - 4 remain after transition at centers (19.5,12.5), (19.5,36.5), (25.5,42.5), (37.5,42.5)', 'w': 'light gray grid cells (16 pixels each)', 'B': 'black L-shaped border separating two grid regions', 'b': 'blue thin accent borders around grids', 'P_or_M': 'purple/pink player avatar with white center - appears at bottom-left after phase transition', 'W': 'white inner background', 'S': 'sky blue outer background'}
- HUD map: {'top edge row 0': 'HUD element that changes on click'}
- Win pattern: Unknown - likely navigate avatar to all 4 gray ring targets
- Cross-level notes: Level 1: two grid regions (top-left 3x7, bottom-right 6x3) separated by black L-border. 5 green plus objects initially at (19,18), (19,24), (19,36), (25,42), (37,42). Phase 1: click green objects then click gray ring to transition. Phase 2: navigate avatar to 4 target positions.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 49 of about 81 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Key discovery! From the rightmost position (34.5, 56.5), UP moved the avatar to (28.5, 56.5) - so UP works at the rightmost position! The avatar moved up 6 rows.
So the avatar can:
- Move LEFT/RIGHT along the corridor at row 34.5
- Move UP from the rightmost position (56.5) to row 28.5
This suggests the black border forms a maze-like path. Let me explore more:
- From (28.5, 56.5), can I move LEFT? That would take me along the top part of the path.
- Can I go further UP from (28.5, 56.5)?
Let me explore the full path systematically. The avatar seems to be navigating a maze formed by the black borders. I need to find all reachable positions and see which ones are adjacent to green objects.
Current position: (34.5, 44.5) after moving LEFT twice and UP (which didn't work there).
Let me go back to the rightmost position and explore upward, then leftward from there.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Avatar at (34.5, 44.5). UP worked from rightmost (56.5) to (28.5, 56.5)
# Need to explore the full maze path

# Go to rightmost first
action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])  # 44.5 -> 50.5 -> 56.5

# Now explore UP from rightmost
positions = []
for i in range(10):
    action(['UP'])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'Y':
            rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
            cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
            center = ((min(rows)+max(rows))/2, (min(cols)+max(cols))/2)
            positions.append(center)
            break
print(f"UP from rightmost: {positions}")

# Now try LEFT from top-right
for i in range(10):
    action(['LEFT'])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'Y':
            rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
            cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
            center = ((min(rows)+max(rows))/2, (min(cols)+max(cols))/2)
            print(f"LEFT: {center}")
            break

# Check colors
seg = current_frame.segmentation
colors = {}
for n in seg['nodes']:
    colors[n['color']] = colors.get(n['color'], 0) + 1
print(f"Colors: {colors}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"LEFT/RIGHT": "Moves avatar horizontally VERIFIED", "UP from rightmost (56.5)": "Moves avatar UP to row 28.5 VERIFIED(step 79)", "DOWN": "Moves avatar DOWN VERIFIED"}, "object_taxonomy": {"N": "green plus objects", "Y": "yellow avatar", "O": "orange avatar inner"}, "win_pattern": "Remove all green objects", "level_log": {"Level 1": "Removed 5 green objects", "Level 2": "Exploring maze path"}, "cross_level_notes": "Level 2: avatar navigates maze formed by black borders"}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Avatar navigates a maze path. Can move UP from rightmost position.", "goal_model": "Navigate to green objects and remove them", "recent_findings": ["UP works from (34.5, 56.5) to (28.5, 56.5)"], "open_questions": ["Full maze path?", "How to interact with green objects?"], "current_plan": "Explore full maze path from rightmost position", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
